title: Merck — RSV — Latest Briefing
html:
Enflonsia Briefing: Q1 2026 (Jan-Mar 2026)

Governing Thought

Q1 '26 is the most data-rich quarter yet for Enflonsia: PET Wave 2 delivered comparative promotional effectiveness data, the digital tracker provided a second wave of HCP behavioral data, and Merck announced positive SMART trial results for second-season use. The picture that emerges is one of a product with strong message resonance and growing digital presence, but still fundamentally constrained by a reach deficit and mid-season switching inertia. The single most important data point: the "committed to Beyfortus" barrier dropped 26 points -- signaling that the door to practice-level switching is opening for the 2026-27 season.

Key Events This Quarter

Date Event Significance
Jan 2026 CDC childhood immunization schedule changes take effect Creates confusion; AAP diverges from CDC guidance
Jan 22 - Mar 18, 2026 PET Wave 2 fielding (Q1 '26) Richest competitive data set to date
Jan-Mar 2026 Digital Tracker Wave 2 fielding Second digital behavioral data wave
Feb 19, 2026 Merck announces positive SMART trial data Second-season use in high-risk children <2 years supported; filing with FDA planned
Q1 2026 2025-26 RSV season winds down End of Enflonsia's first competitive season

PET Wave 2 Deep Dive (Q1 '26)

Promotional Reach: Gap Persists

Metric Enflonsia Beyfortus Gap
PP Reach (% MDs) 50% 80% -30 pts
PP Frequency (visits/HCP/30 days) 1.1 1.3 -0.2
NPP Reach 41% 95% -54 pts
NPP Frequency 3.8 5.0 -1.2

Enflonsia's SoV increased directionally from Q4 '25 but Beyfortus maintained dominant reach. ~90% of Enflonsia interactions were in-person; 28% were first-time meetings (down 28pts from Q4, meaning reps are deepening existing relationships rather than expanding reach).

Message Performance: Enflonsia's Messages Are Working

Top Enflonsia messages (Q1 '26):

Rank ID Message ME Score Recall Trend vs. Q4
1 ENF2 Same dose regardless of weight 79% 84% +12% recall
2 ENF10 Single 105 mg IM injection 73% 69% +17% recall
3 ENF8 30-month shelf life 68% 63% +14% recall
4 ENF9 48-hour room temp stability 65% 42% Stable
5 ENF3 84.3% efficacy against hospitalization 60% 56% Stable

Key insight: ENF2 + ENF10 delivered in combination correlates with stronger rep evaluations, product perception, AND stocking intent. This combination is the optimal call strategy.

Top Beyfortus messages (Q1 '26):

Rank ID Message ME Score Recall Trend vs. Q4
1 BEYF1 First long-acting antibody indication 72% 48% +8% ME
2 BEYF12 6M+ infants, real-world data 67% 35% Stable
3 BEYF3 74.9% efficacy reduction 67% 52% Stable
4 BEYF10 Unparalleled body of RWE 66% 30% +26% ME
5 BEYF13 Indicated through second RSV season 62% 62% +6% ME

Key insight: Beyfortus campaign message (BEYF10 -- RWE emphasis) surged +26% in effectiveness. Real-world evidence is becoming more persuasive over time, widening Enflonsia's vulnerability.

HCP Perceptions: Enflonsia's Operational Edge Holds

Enflonsia significantly higher than Beyfortus:

  • Dosing & administration
  • Shelf-life requirements
  • Hours out of cold storage
  • Operational benefits (stocking/implementation ease)

Beyfortus significantly higher than Enflonsia:

  • Efficacy
  • Safety

Declining for Enflonsia vs. Q4 '25:

  • Overall impression (-8%)
  • Ease of ordering (-7%)
  • Cost/coverage (-16%)
  • Efficacy perception (-9%)
  • Safety perception (-13%)

The declines in cost/coverage and efficacy perception are concerning. HCPs are hearing more about Enflonsia but becoming less convinced on the dimensions where Beyfortus leads.

Stocking Likelihood: The Critical Metric

Current season (Oct '25-Mar '26):

  • ~33% of HCPs report high likelihood to stock Enflonsia (vs. Beyfortus significantly higher)
  • Beyfortus continued to lead anticipated prescribing share

Barriers to stocking (Q1 '26 vs. Q4 '25):

Barrier Trend Signal
Committed to/using Beyfortus -26% POSITIVE -- door opening
Financial uncertainty +8% Negative -- cost/access conversations needed
Practice policy: stock one product only +10% Structural -- hard to change
Too late in season NEW Timing -- resolves by pre-season
Need more information Stable Rep training opportunity

Next season (Oct '26-Mar '27): Top barrier remains "happy with/unwilling to change from Beyfortus," but this question was asked for the first time in Q1 '26, so no trend data yet.

Rep Performance: Still Trailing

  • Enflonsia reps comparable on overall call quality
  • Trail Beyfortus on: knowledge about RSV preventives (-6%), credible support for claims (-9%), addressing questions (-6%), compelling reason to recommend (-6%)
  • Trust in Merck reps declined vs. Q4 '25 (-14%)
  • Best-in-class perception declined (-15%)
  • HCPs want Enflonsia reps to: clarify coverage details, differentiate from Beyfortus, lead with evidence

HCP Advice for Enflonsia Reps

Top asks from HCPs (Q1 '26):

  1. Clarify coverage details
  2. Show cost savings
  3. Differentiate from Beyfortus (increased +16% vs. Q4)
  4. Lead with evidence
  5. Provide patient materials

Digital Tracker Wave 2 (Q1 '26)

What Improved

  • Paid search share increased -- Enflonsia gained on both general RSV and Beyfortus keyword terms
  • Banner ad share increased -- From near-zero to roughly equal Beyfortus in ad exposures (both at 3% reach, ~3 ads/HCP)
  • Email content streamlined -- Focused on coverage information and immunization logistics

What Didn't

  • Beyfortus leads in product website browsing -- HCPs spend more time on Beyfortus sites
  • Beyfortus email content rated more relevant -- Beyfortus's varied campaigns (RSV alerts, parent messaging, seasonal reminders) outperform Enflonsia's operational emails
  • Banner ad reach still abysmal for both -- 94% of HCPs saw zero RSV market-basket ads

Paid Search Detail (Q1 '26)

  • Enflonsia: 22% SoE on general RSV terms (up from 12% in Q4 '25)
  • Enflonsia: 27% SoE on Beyfortus keyword searches
  • Beyfortus: 37% SoE on own keyword searches (down from dominance)
  • Strategy is working: Enflonsia is intercepting category-seeking and competitor-seeking HCPs

SMART Trial: Second-Season Data

What Happened

Merck announced (Feb 19, 2026) positive Phase 3 SMART trial data for Enflonsia in children under 2 years at increased risk for severe RSV through their second RSV season.

Key Results

  • Achieved serum concentrations over a second RSV season similar to those in healthy infants in the pivotal CANOPY trial
  • Safety profile generally consistent with first-season data
  • Data presented at 9th RSVVW Conference

Why It Matters

  • Beyfortus already has second-season indication for high-risk children <24 months
  • Enflonsia currently limited to first-season use
  • SMART data supports filing for label expansion
  • If approved, removes one of Beyfortus's remaining structural advantages
  • Filing timeline not yet announced -- urgency is high

CDC Schedule Changes Impact

HCP Awareness and Response (from PET Q1 '26)

  • 65% of HCPs aware of Jan 2026 CDC immunization schedule changes
  • ~70% say their RSV recommendation will not change
  • ~15% will recommend RSV prevention to more patients
  • ~15% will recommend to fewer patients

But the Market Is Shrinking

  • 2/3 of HCPs expect fewer infants will receive RSV prevention overall
  • Primary driver: increased parent refusal (not reduced HCP advocacy)
  • HCPs will prioritize high-risk infants when facing parent pushback
  • Clinical advocacy and reliance on AAP/AAFP (over CDC) are sustaining factors

HCP Trust Hierarchy

  1. AAP/AAFP recommendations (most trusted)
  2. Clinical evidence / peer-reviewed literature
  3. CDC/ACIP guidelines (declining, perceived as "politically motivated" by 25% of HCPs)

Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
Sanofi/AZ Beyfortus (nirsevimab) ~$250M Moderate Late season; supply improved; targeting 80% coverage for 2025-26 season
Pfizer Abrysvo (RSVpreF) ~$80M Declining Off-season; maternal approach conceded; pivoting to older adults
GSK Arexvy (RSV, older adults) ~$250M Growing Durability data vs. competitors strengthening position
Enflonsia Season winding down — PET Wave 2 data rich; SMART trial positive; stocking barrier declining

Sanofi/AstraZeneca — Beyfortus (~$250M)

Sanofi Q1 2026 earnings commentary:

  1. Beyfortus targeting 80% eligible infant coverage for the 2025-26 season. Sanofi's stated goal of 80% coverage (up from 60%+ in accessible markets in the prior season) would leave limited room for Enflonsia to capture new patients. However, this is an aspiration — actual coverage will be validated by CDC data later in 2026.

  2. Second RSV season planning underway with supply locked in. Sanofi emphasized early contracting and reservation programs for the 2026-27 season. This preemptive stocking strategy directly competes with Enflonsia's pre-season conversion window (July-September 2026).

  3. Enflonsia's FDA approval acknowledged as first direct competition. Sanofi's language shifted from ignoring Enflonsia to explicitly noting "the competitive monoclonal landscape." This is a significant change — Sanofi now treats Enflonsia as a named competitor rather than a theoretical threat.

Implication for Merck: The 80% coverage target is the number to watch. If Beyfortus achieves it, Enflonsia's addressable market narrows to the 20% of practices not yet committed — plus any practices willing to switch. The 26-point decline in "committed to Beyfortus" barriers (from PET data) suggests the switching pool is larger than Sanofi's coverage target implies. ZoomRx's pre-season stocking intent data (Q2-Q3 2026) will be the definitive arbiter.

Pfizer — Abrysvo (~$80M)

Pfizer Q1 2026 earnings commentary:

  1. Pfizer acknowledged that the maternal vaccination approach is losing to monoclonals. This is now explicit in Pfizer's communications — a significant concession. The maternal approach had been Pfizer's primary infant RSV strategy, and its failure validates the Beyfortus/Enflonsia mechanism class.

  2. Older adult RSV market growing but crowded. Abrysvo faces Arexvy (GSK) and mResvia (Moderna) in the 60+ market. Off-season revenue (~$80M) reflects the non-seasonal nature of adult vaccination vs. the sharp seasonality of infant RSV.

Implication for Merck: Abrysvo's retreat from infant RSV removes the "three-way" narrative. The story is definitively Enflonsia vs. Beyfortus. Merck can now frame competitive messaging exclusively against one incumbent rather than defending against multiple mechanisms.

GSK — Arexvy (~$250M)

GSK continues to grow Arexvy in the older adult market (~$250M in Q1, off-season). Durability data is being positioned against Abrysvo and mResvia. Arexvy remains irrelevant to Enflonsia's infant market but demonstrates the long-term revenue potential of RSV prevention products.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. The infant RSV competitive landscape is now definitively a two-product market. Pfizer has conceded the maternal approach. Enflonsia's competitive positioning should be entirely focused on Beyfortus — dosing simplicity, shelf life, and room temp stability vs. Beyfortus's RWE, established relationships, and second-season indication.

  2. Sanofi is playing defense by locking in 2026-27 season commitments early. Enflonsia's window to convert practices is July-September 2026 — the pre-season stocking window. Any delay in rep deployment, access messaging, or SMART trial filing reduces the addressable switching pool.

  3. Enflonsia's SMART trial data (second-season use) is the competitive equalizer. Beyfortus's second-season indication is one of its structural advantages. If Merck files and gains approval before the 2026-27 season, it removes this differentiator. If not, Beyfortus retains an edge for high-risk infants requiring multi-season protection.

  4. The "committed to Beyfortus" barrier declining 26 points is the single most important competitive signal — more important than any revenue figure. It means practice-level switching intent is growing, and the 2026-27 pre-season is genuinely contestable.

So What for ZoomRx

For the Merck Account Team

  1. Lead with the "committed to Beyfortus" decline story -- This is the most client-relevant finding. The door is opening for 2026-27 season conversions.
  2. Flag the rep performance gap -- Merck needs to hear that their reps trail Beyfortus across credibility and question-handling metrics. This is fixable with training.
  3. Position cost/access as the urgent messaging gap -- The 16% decline in cost/coverage perception combined with HCPs asking for coverage clarity = actionable finding.
  4. SMART trial creates a strategic inflection -- When Merck files for second-season indication, PET should track whether this changes the "need Beyfortus for high-risk" barrier.

For PET Study Design (Q2 '26 Wave)

  1. Track pre-season stocking decisions explicitly -- July-September is the critical window. Add questions about order placement timing and switching intent.
  2. Monitor ENF2 ceiling effect -- If recall plateaus above 80%, the question becomes whether secondary messages can gain traction.
  3. Add RWE readiness question -- "If Enflonsia published real-world evidence showing comparable outcomes to Beyfortus, how would this change your stocking decision?" This forward-looking question has high client value.

For Digital Tracker

  1. Continue tracking paid search conquest strategy -- Enflonsia's Beyfortus keyword bidding is the smartest digital tactic in the data. Track effectiveness over time.
  2. Watch for pre-season digital surge -- July-September digital activity should spike. Capture this window explicitly.

So What for ZoomRx

Monitor ENF2 ceiling effect -- If recall plateaus above 80%, the question becomes whether secondary messages can gain traction.
Add RWE readiness question -- "If Enflonsia published real-world evidence showing comparable outcomes to Beyfortus, how would this change your stocking decision?" This forward-looking question has high client value.

For Digital Tracker
